column43: The environmental changes were very effective and helped increase immersion. Made it feel more realistic as they represented an individual's facial expressions which would be present in a real case scenario.
column44: I could better understand how my questions were making the agent "feel". The angry or "red" environment state let me know that I was getting closer to breaking the agent into confessing to the crime.  This motivated me to progress in the game as I know that I was getting closer to winning.
column45: Not really. It simply added to the experience.
column46: Show when the agent is "thinking", i.e. generating an answer. And maybe also indicate that when you are speaking to the agent, your words are being heard by the system. So that you would be aware if the system did not manage to hear your words properly.
column47:
The emotional aspect helps you get more invested in the game. The level of immersion is much greater and helps to get you hooked into the game. 

The interaction that excluded emotional aspects was less close to a realistic scenario of being able to perceive ones emotions. This meant it was more challenging to know what the "agent" was thinking and so you could not know if you were on the right track with the questions you were asking. 

However, the main difference would be the level of immersion experienced specifically in the emotional interaction which I felt took the game to another level.
